planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants level with the ground and water them in to settle. This compact, well-branched hybrid blooms heavily and reblooms with deadheading. Full spikes of pink flowers cover the mounded plant through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and water in until roots take hold. Removing spent spikes keeps the plant tidy and encourages repeat bloom. Slim upright spikes of pure white flowers rise above the foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in deep, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant this tall selection in rich, evenly moist ground where the strong stems stand without staking. Give it space so the whorled foliage and architectural form show. Branching spires of lavender-blue flowers, sometimes curved, open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil for a tall accent.

directions: Set divisions in deep moist soil where the upright stems will not need support. Leave the seed heads standing for winter structure after bloom. Slender candelabras of clean white flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, retentive soil with room to grow tall.

directions: Plant this vigorous selection in deep moist soil where its height will not need staking. Space it generously so the tiered foliage is shown to advantage. Tall spires of soft lavender flowers rise in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile ground.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, evenly moist soil so the tall stems stand tall on their own. Allow room for air movement around the whorled foliage. Gently arching spikes of pale pink flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set this white-flowered selection in rich moist soil where the sturdy stems need no staking. Space plants so the architectural form reads well in the border. Slim white candelabras of flowers open in mid to late summer over whorled leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, retentive soil.

directions: Plant in deep moist soil so the upright stems remain self-supporting. Give it room and leave the dried spikes for winter interest. Branching spires of lilac-blue flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in full sun.

directions: Set divisions in deep retentive soil where the tall stems stand without support. Full sun keeps the flower color strongest. Tapering spires of soft pink flowers rise above whorled foliage in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Plant divisions in rich moist soil so the strong stems need no staking. Space generously and allow the tiered leaves to show. Slender spires of pale rose-pink flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall for cold stratification or start indoors after chilling in spring.

directions: Sow shallowly into moist soil, giving seed a cold, damp period to break dormancy. This tough native tolerates lawns, shade, and clay, self-seeding freely once established. Heart-shaped foliage and violet-blue blooms appear from early to mid spring.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant out a couple of weeks before the last frost.

directions: Cover the seed lightly and germinate in the dark, as darkness improves the sprouting rate. Shear plants back by half after the first flush to keep them tidy and encourage rebloom. The long-spurred flowers carry on from spring well into summer where nights stay cool.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall for a wave of self-sown seedlings.

directions: Press the tiny seed into moist soil and cover lightly, as it germinates best in darkness. Plants are short-lived but reseed generously, popping up in beds and paths year after year. The purple, yellow, and white faces bloom from spring through early summer and again in cool fall weather.

planting.note: Start indoors in cool conditions and set out hardened plants a few weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow into a cool flat and cover to exclude light, keeping temperatures moderate for even sprouting. Deadhead often and feed lightly to keep the large blossoms coming through cool weather. Grown as a fall-to-spring bedding plant in mild zones, it fades once summer heat sets in.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall so winter cold breaks its stubborn dormancy.

directions: Sow into lean, sharply drained sand or gravel where this fussy native resents rich, wet ground. The finely divided leaves resemble a bird's foot beneath large, flat, pansy-like flowers. Blooms open in mid to late spring; keep crowns dry through dormancy to prevent rot.

planting.note: Sow after a cold period or simply let established plants self-seed into shade.

directions: Give this violet moist, shaded soil where its purple-flushed foliage colors best. It spreads readily by seed and makes a low carpet under shrubs and trees. Small lavender flowers dot the dark leaves through spring.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed outdoors in fall to satisfy its need for cold stratification.

directions: Sow into humus-rich woodland soil in a cool, shaded spot. This taller native carries white flowers with a yellow throat and a purple reverse. Blooms appear from spring into early summer above leafy, stemmy growth.

planting.note: Fall sow into a sunny, well-drained bed to break its dormancy over winter.

directions: Sow into lean, well-drained prairie soil in full sun. Its deeply cut, fan-shaped leaves distinguish it from more common blue violets. Purple flowers appear in mid to late spring above the fine foliage.

planting.note: Sow after cold stratification into a consistently moist, low spot.

directions: Plant in wet meadows, rain gardens, or pond edges where the soil stays damp. Blue-violet flowers are held on tall stalks above the leaves, with a dark eye at the center. It flowers in mid to late spring and tolerates standing moisture.

planting.note: Sow fresh in fall so the seed overwinters and germinates in spring.

directions: Sow into sandy or loamy soil in sun to light shade. Named for its arrow-shaped leaves, it carries deep violet flowers close to the ground. Blooms appear in mid spring on this compact native.

planting.note: Fall sow in a cool, shaded, moist site typical of its native streamsides.

directions: Plant in cool, wet woodland soil in shade along seeps and stream edges. Bright yellow flowers with brown pencil lines rise above heart-shaped leaves. This western native blooms in spring and spreads by rhizomes.

planting.note: Sow after a cold period or allow the plants to self-seed in shade.

directions: Sow into moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. This scentless European violet bears blue-violet flowers with a pale spur. It flowers through spring and naturalizes easily in woodland gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in cool soil.

directions: Scatter seed on open, disturbed ground and press in lightly. This small annual pansy bears creamy-white flowers with a touch of yellow. It flowers over a long spring and summer season and reseeds itself.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling and plant out into cool, gritty soil.

directions: Sow into gritty, well-drained soil suited to its upland origins. Bright yellow flowers, sometimes flushed violet, sit close over spreading mats. It blooms through late spring and summer in cool climates.

planting.note: Most easily grown by dividing runners and setting them in moist shade in spring.

directions: Set divisions into moist, shaded soil where the runners can spread into a dense groundcover. The small white-and-mauve flowers hover on thin stems above kidney-shaped leaves. It flowers over much of the year in mild frost-free climates.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant into free-draining soil.

directions: Sow lightly covered and grow on in gritty, well-drained soil in sun. This long-blooming Mediterranean species bears deep violet pansy-faced flowers. It flowers from spring right through to autumn and is generously self-seeding.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings or division since this named viola does not come true from seed.

directions: Set out young plants raised from cuttings into fertile, well-drained soil. This classic selection is prized for its near-black flowers with a small yellow eye. Shear it back after the first flush to keep the dark blooms coming through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors; it comes fairly true and self-seeds freely.

directions: Press the small seed into moist soil and keep it dark until sprouts appear. This heirloom Johnny jump-up carries velvety black flowers with a tiny golden center. It blooms from spring into summer and drops seed for the following year.

planting.note: Grow from cuttings or division to keep this old selection true.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in rich, moisture-retentive soil. This cheerful heirloom shows chocolate-brown upper petals over bright yellow lower ones. Deadhead and cut back midseason to prolong the long display of two-toned faces.

planting.note: Propagate by cuttings or division, as it will not come true from seed.

directions: Set out young plants into fertile, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This beloved old viola bears fragrant silvery-mauve flowers with a creamy throat. Regular deadheading and a midsummer trim keep it flowering for months.

planting.note: Increase by cuttings or division to keep the unusual coloring true.

directions: Grow rooted cuttings on in fertile, well-drained soil and shelter from harsh sun. Its curious khaki-gold and bronze flowers with a dark eye are unlike any other viola. Cut back and feed after the first flush to renew the quirky blooms.

planting.note: Grow from cuttings or division so the two-tone flowers stay consistent.

directions: Set out young plants in rich, moist, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This favorite selection carries soft cream flowers edged with lavender-purple. Shear it back after the main flush to bring on a fresh wave of scented blooms.

planting.note: Propagate by cuttings or division to keep the ruffled markings true.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in fertile, moist soil in sun or dappled shade. This scented viola shows cream flowers speckled and streaked with violet at the edges. Deadhead often and trim midseason for a long, fragrant show.

planting.note: Grow from cuttings or division so the streaked pattern comes true.

directions: Set young plants into moist, fertile, well-drained soil. Its white flowers are boldly streaked and splashed with purple-blue, no two quite alike. Shear back after the first flush to encourage months of blooms.

planting.note: Divide clumps or let it self-seed, though seedlings vary in spotting.

directions: Set divisions into moist, humus-rich soil in sun to part shade. This selection of the common blue violet has white flowers stippled all over with blue speckles. It blooms in spring and self-sows into charming, freckled colonies.

planting.note: Divide established clumps or allow it to self-seed in shade.

directions: Set divisions into rich, moist soil in dappled shade or sun. This form bears grayish-white flowers heavily veined with blue-violet toward the center. It flowers in early to mid spring and naturalizes into a soft groundcover.

planting.note: Divide runners or set out young plants to keep the color true.

directions: Plant divisions in rich, moist soil in dappled shade. This scented sweet violet bears deep blue-violet flowers on long stems, good for tiny posies. The strongly fragrant blooms open in early spring with an occasional fall repeat.

planting.note: Divide runners in early spring or fall to keep the white form true.

directions: Set divisions into moist, humus-rich soil in light shade. This white-flowered sweet violet is prized for its clean color and strong perfume. Fragrant blooms appear in early spring above tidy heart-shaped foliage.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; it self-seeds reliably once established.

directions: Press seed into moist soil and keep dark until germination. This vigorous Johnny jump-up shows the classic purple, lavender, and yellow tricolor face. It blooms freely from spring into summer and reseeds itself year after year.

planting.note: Grow from cuttings or division to keep the soft blue color consistent.

directions: Set young plants into fertile, moist, well-drained soil. This selection bears dainty sky-blue flowers over a long season and mounding foliage. Trim it back after the first flush to keep the cool blue blooms coming.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings and set out young plants in spring.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in fertile, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This trailing viola smothers itself in deep purple flowers over a long season. Shear it lightly midseason to renew the cascade of bloom.

planting.note: Grow from cuttings or division to keep the pale coloring true.

directions: Set out young plants in moist, fertile, well-drained soil. This gentle selection bears soft creamy-white flowers washed faintly with pale blue. Deadhead and cut back midseason to keep the delicate blooms coming.

planting.note: Sow fresh in fall to give the seed its needed winter chilling.

directions: Sow into moist, humus-rich woodland soil in part shade. This East Asian violet bears pale violet flowers, and some forms carry purple-tinted foliage. It flowers in spring and self-seeds gently into shady borders.

planting.note: Increase by division to keep the silvery foliage form true.

directions: Set divisions into moist, well-drained woodland soil in part shade. This creeping violet is grown for its silver-veined foliage as much as its lavender flowers. It spreads by trailing stems to form a low, ornamental mat and blooms in spring.

planting.note: Start indoors in cool conditions and set out hardened plants before the last frost.

directions: Sow into a cool flat and cover to block light for even germination. This old strain produces very large, richly blotched flowers in many colors. Deadhead regularly for a long cool-season show from fall through spring in mild areas.

planting.note: Divide clumps to keep the reddish color, as seedlings may vary.

directions: Set divisions into moist, humus-rich soil in sun to part shade. This form of the common blue violet bears warm reddish-purple flowers. It blooms in spring and naturalizes readily into an easy groundcover.

planting.note: Sow after cold stratification or let it self-seed in woodland shade.

directions: Sow into cool, moist, leafy soil in shade. This woodland violet opens its slender lilac flowers earlier than the common dog violet, with a narrow darker spur. It flowers in early spring and settles quietly into shady borders.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall for edible, self-seeding flowers.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the seed dark until it sprouts. This old cottage-garden violet has small, edible tricolor flowers good for salads and garnishes. It blooms from spring into summer and self-seeds throughout the garden.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the surrounding soil and firm it in well, then water to settle the roots. Shear lightly in early spring to keep the mound dense and encourage fresh flowering wood. Large deep-yellow flowers open from early summer and keep coming until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in any well-drained garden soil.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the rootball and backfill with the native soil, watering deeply after planting. Trim back a third of the older stems in spring to renew the blue-green foliage. Pure white saucer flowers cover the bush from early summer through autumn.

planting.note: Spring planting suits this small-leaved, fine-textured form.

directions: Position the plant at its original growing depth and water in thoroughly. Give it a light spring haircut to maintain the neat rounded habit and dense small leaves. Bright buttercup-yellow blooms appear steadily from June until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; flower color holds best out of the hottest afternoon sun.

directions: Plant into well-drained ground and mulch lightly to keep the roots cool. A spot with morning sun and some afternoon shade keeps the warm tones from bleaching. Copper-orange flushed blooms fade to soft yellow and repeat from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Set out in spring where it gets cool roots and bright light.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly through the first summer. Cooler weather and steady moisture keep the flowers their deepest color. Vermilion-red blooms with a yellow eye open in early summer and are richest in the cooler days of the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the fullest flowering.

directions: Backfill around the rootball with unamended garden soil and firm gently before watering. Prune out a few of the oldest stems each spring to keep the arching mound tidy. Soft primrose-yellow flowers set against gray-green leaves bloom from June to autumn.

planting.note: Give this larger cultivar room to arch when planting in spring.

directions: Allow generous space for the graceful arching branches and plant at the original soil depth. A light spring trim keeps growth balanced without spoiling the fountain shape. Lemon-yellow flowers stud the branches from early summer well into fall.

planting.note: An extra-hardy selection; plant in spring in cold-climate gardens.

directions: Set the plant slightly high in heavy soils to ensure sharp drainage, then water in. This tough selection needs little beyond a spring tidy-up of old wood. Bright yellow flowers cover the rounded bush from early summer until frost.

planting.note: A prairie-bred cultivar; plant in spring in open, sunny sites.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist while establishing. Clip lightly in spring to encourage a compact, floriferous habit. Rich golden-yellow flowers bloom heavily from early summer through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring where afternoon shade keeps the pink color true.

directions: Set out in well-drained soil and mulch to keep the root zone cool. A little afternoon shade in hot regions prevents the flowers from fading toward white. Semi-double clear pink blooms appear from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant this compact bicolor in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the original depth in well-drained ground and water in well. Its dwarf, dense habit needs only a light spring shaping. Yellow flowers brushed with orange-red centers cover the mound from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; warm flower tones deepen in cool weather.

directions: Set into well-drained soil and keep watered through the first season. Shear lightly each spring to maintain the tidy dwarf shape. Warm orange-yellow flowers are richest in the cool days of early summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant this semi-double white form in spring.

directions: Backfill with native soil and firm around the roots before a deep watering. Remove a few old stems in spring to keep the bush open and vigorous. Semi-double snow-white flowers bloom generously from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant level with the soil surface in a well-drained bed and water thoroughly. A light spring trim keeps the mound compact and free-flowering. Soft creamy-white blooms open from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Use this low spreading form along fronts of beds; plant in spring.

directions: Set plants at the front of a border and water in to settle the spreading roots. Its low, trailing habit works well over walls and needs only occasional tidying. Bright yellow flowers with a slightly ruffled edge bloom from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a broad, dome-shaped bush.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and firm the roots before watering deeply. This dependable dome benefits from thinning a few old stems each spring. Large canary-yellow flowers smother the plant from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for unusually large flowers.

directions: Set out in a sunny, well-drained spot and water through establishment. A light spring cut-back keeps the low bush neat and full of bloom. Exceptionally large flat yellow flowers appear from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: A dwarf cushion form; plant in spring for edging and low hedges.

directions: Plant at close spacing for a low informal hedge and water in well. The dense cushion habit needs only a light shear in spring. Small bright yellow flowers cover the compact mound from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Give this taller, upright form room when planting in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained garden soil at the original depth and water deeply. Remove a portion of the oldest wood in spring to keep the upright bush vigorous. Deep yellow flowers bloom abundantly from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; warm hues hold best with some afternoon shade.

directions: Set into well-drained soil and mulch to moderate root temperature. Light afternoon shade in hot climates keeps the flame tones from bleaching. Orange to brick-red flowers shift with the weather and bloom from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring where cooler air preserves the soft peach color.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water regularly during the first summer. A little afternoon shade keeps the delicate coloring from fading. Peachy-pink flowers with a cream base bloom from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give it cool roots to hold the orange tone.

directions: Set out in gritty, well-drained soil and keep moist while establishing. Mulch and light afternoon shade help keep the flower color deep. Bright orange blooms with a darker eye appear from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the cleanest pale flowers.

directions: Plant at the original depth in well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. Shear lightly in spring to keep the rounded form dense. Soft pale-yellow flowers with deeper yellow centers bloom from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter, then set plants out after frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and barely cover, keeping the tray at room temperature until sprouts show. Move seedlings to a sunny, well-drained bed and space them for good airflow. Sprays of cherry-rose flowers with darker eyes appear from early to midsummer above strawberry-like leaves.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface of moist mix and keep warm until germination. Grow on in full sun with sharp drainage and avoid waterlogged soil in winter. Deep rose-pink flowers with a crimson center dot the compact clump through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; transplant out once frosts have passed.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it lightly moist and warm until seedlings emerge. Plant into a sunny, well-drained border where the silvery-backed foliage can spread into a low clump. Blood-red to crimson flowers rise on branching stems through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pot-grown plants in spring; does not come true from seed.

directions: Set plants in fertile, well-drained soil with the crown at the surface and water in. Divide established clumps in spring every few years to keep them vigorous. Vivid single scarlet flowers blaze over the strawberry-like foliage through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring from divisions; propagate by splitting the clump.

directions: Plant in a sunny border in soil that drains freely and never sits wet. Lift and divide the crown in spring to renew flowering vigor. Semi-double flame-orange flowers flecked with yellow bloom from early to late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the tray warm and evenly moist until it sprouts. Grow in full sun with good drainage for the strongest flower color. Rich wine-red flowers with a nearly black center bloom on wiry stems all summer.

planting.note: Direct-sow around the last frost in a sunny, open spot.

directions: Scatter seed on prepared ground and rake in lightly, then keep moist until seedlings appear. Once established it tolerates poor, dry soils with little care. Pale sulphur-yellow flowers open in flat clusters through early and midsummer; deadhead to limit self-seeding.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring as a low mat between stones.

directions: Sow seed shallowly or set small plants into gritty soil and firm them in. This tough mat spreads to knit between paving and over gentle slopes with little water. Bright yellow star flowers cover the ground-hugging foliage in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for a tight, low carpet.

directions: Space plants closely in lean, well-drained soil and water until rooted. This very flat form takes light foot traffic and needs almost no maintenance. Small golden-yellow flowers stud the tight green mat in spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out after frosts for a low mounded clump.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm until the seedlings appear, then grow on in bright light. Set into well-drained soil where the soft, hairy leaves form a neat clump. Large single yellow flowers rise just above the woolly foliage through early summer.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in lean, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in gritty, acidic soil and keep lightly moist while it germinates. This native mat thrives in poor, rocky ground and needs no feeding. Clusters of small white flowers appear in early summer above glossy leaves that redden in fall.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring in dry, sunny ground.

directions: Scatter seed on lean soil and press in lightly, keeping just moist until it sprouts. Once rooted it endures heat and drought in the poorest ground. Small yellow flowers appear through summer above leaves that are silvery-white beneath.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; tolerates light shade when planted out.

directions: Sow seed and cover thinly, giving it a period of cool moisture to break dormancy if needed. Plant into humus-rich soil in dappled light where it slowly forms a low mat. Pure white five-petaled flowers open in spring above dark green palmate leaves.

planting.note: Sow in spring in moist, acidic soil, including boggy edges.

directions: Sow shallowly in damp, acidic ground and keep the surface from drying out. This wiry native tolerates poor, moist soils and needs little attention. Distinctive four-petaled yellow flowers dot the sprawling stems all through summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring; vigorous runners fill space quickly, so site with care.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep moist; expect fast, spreading growth once established. Runners root at the nodes to blanket bare ground, so confine it away from choice plantings. Bright yellow flowers appear on slender stalks throughout summer.

planting.note: Start indoors; a chilling period aids germination. Ideal for rock gardens.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist spell before warming to prompt even germination. Grow in gritty, sharply drained soil in a rock garden or trough. Yellow flowers, often with an orange spot at the base of each petal, open in early summer.

planting.note: Raise indoors and plant out in spring; excellent for alpine beds.

directions: Surface-sow and cool-stratify if germination is slow, then grow on in bright light. Plant in lean, gritty soil where it forms a tidy low cushion. Golden-yellow flowers with a deeper eye cover the mat in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring; a good native for meadows and dry borders.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on prepared ground and keep just moist until seedlings appear. This western native tolerates drought once its deep roots establish. Airy sprays of clear yellow flowers rise on slender stems through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall as a shade groundcover.

directions: Set divisions into humus-rich soil with the crown level and water until established. Spreading runners knit together into a dense, weed-suppressing evergreen mat under trees. Bright yellow strawberry-like flowers appear in spring above glossy three-part leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in dappled woodland shade.

directions: Plant into moist, well-drained woodland soil rich in leaf mold and water in. This native groundcover slowly spreads into an evergreen carpet that shrugs off dry summer shade. Small yellow flowers rise above the strawberry-like leaves in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant clumps in spring in partial shade with steady moisture.

directions: Set the clump into humus-rich, well-drained soil and keep it evenly moist. Unlike its runnering relatives it forms a tidy non-spreading mound of lobed leaves. Loose sprays of yellow flowers appear above the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about two months before the last frost and set plants out once nights stay warm.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of moist mix and leave it uncovered, since light triggers germination. Pinch young plants once to build a bushy habit and keep them evenly watered in warm weather. The powder-puff blue flowers open in early summer and repeat until frost if you shear spent clusters.

planting.note: A tender tropical annual set out only after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it lightly moist and warm until sprouts appear. This looser, weedier relative of garden flossflower tolerates heat and poor soil once established. Small pale-blue to whitish tufts appear through summer; deadhead to limit its prolific self-sowing.

planting.note: A tall F1 cut-flower type set out after frost with room to reach full height.

directions: Start seed indoors and press it onto the mix without covering, giving it warmth and light. This tall F1 selection holds long, sturdy stems that make it a favorite for cutting and drying. Cut the lavender-blue clusters when most florets are open to encourage a steady flush of new stems.

planting.note: A classic heirloom bedding type transplanted once soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed on the surface indoors and keep it moist and bright until it sprouts. This long-grown heirloom forms tidy mounds topped with fluffy powder-blue heads. Shear lightly after the first flush to keep it blooming and compact through the season.

planting.note: A compact F1 bedding hybrid set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm, moist mix and do not cover, as it needs light to sprout. This uniform dwarf hybrid keeps a low, even carpet ideal for edging and mass bedding. Lavender-blue tufts cover the plants from early summer to frost with little care.

planting.note: An early, very dwarf F1 series member for warm-weather bedding.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under bright light and keep the mix consistently warm. This early, tightly mounded selection blooms young and stays neat in beds and containers. Mid-blue tufts smother the foliage all summer; a light shearing revives flowering in late season.

planting.note: The white-flowered form of the compact Hawaii series, planted after frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto warm, moist mix and leave it exposed to light. This dwarf selection carries clean creamy-white tufts that brighten edges and mixed pots. Deadhead or shear spent heads to keep the display fresh into autumn.

planting.note: A deep-purple dwarf selection in the Hawaii series set out after frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface indoors and keep warm and bright until germination. This compact form stands out for its rich violet-purple tufts on very uniform plants. Trim lightly after heavy bloom to prompt a strong second flush before frost.

planting.note: A mounding, self-cleaning series often grown from cuttings and transplanted after frost.

directions: Start from seed on the surface of warm mix, or set out rooted plants after frost. This vigorous, weather-tolerant selection spreads into a soft mound of medium-blue tufts. Its self-cleaning habit means it keeps flowering all summer with little deadheading.

planting.note: A purple-flowered mounding selection set out once nights turn warm.

directions: Sow on the surface in warmth and light, or transplant rooted starts after frost. This heat-loving selection forms spreading mounds crowned with violet-purple heads. It self-cleans well, holding color from early summer straight through to first frost.

planting.note: A taller Artist selection for the middle of beds and mixed containers.

directions: Press seed onto warm, moist mix under light, or set out rooted plants after frost. This taller member of the series adds height to combinations while keeping the durable, self-cleaning habit. Blue tufts stand above the foliage and rebloom freely through the warm months.

planting.note: A very early, dwarf F1 bedding series planted after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep the tray warm and well lit for even sprouting. This early, compact selection blooms quickly and stays low and uniform for edging. Medium-blue tufts cover the plants all season with only occasional tidying.

planting.note: The white form of the dwarf Aloha series, transplanted after frost.

directions: Press seed onto warm, moist mix and leave uncovered so light can reach it. This dwarf, early selection offers clean white tufts on compact, even plants. Shear lightly midseason to refresh the flush of bloom into fall.

planting.note: A distinctive bicolor selection set out once the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow on the surface of warm, moist mix and keep bright until germination. This selection is prized for two-tone florets that open blue and white for a frosted look. Keep it deadheaded to maintain the fresh bicolor effect through the growing season.

planting.note: A rounded dwarf bedding selection planted after the last frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto warm mix and leave it uncovered for light. This selection forms neat, ball-shaped mounds densely packed with blue tufts. Trim spent heads to keep the tidy dome flowering well into autumn.

planting.note: A medium-tall type useful for cutting, transplanted after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors on the mix surface with warmth and light. This taller selection produces upright stems well suited to small bouquets. Cut stems when the clusters are mostly open to prompt further branching and bloom.

planting.note: A tall novelty cut-flower type grown for its unusual color, set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep the mix warm and brightly lit until seedlings appear. This tall selection is grown for its unusual reddish-mauve tufts on long, strong stems. Harvest stems for the vase as the heads color up, which also keeps fresh flowers coming.

planting.note: A heat-tolerant F1 bedding hybrid transplanted after frost.

directions: Press seed onto warm, moist mix under light and keep evenly damp. This F1 hybrid holds up well in summer heat and keeps a rounded, floriferous mound. Sky-blue tufts blanket the plant from early summer until frost with minimal upkeep.

planting.note: A very dwarf F1 series for edging and pots, set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors in warmth and light for even germination. This compact series stays low and dense, perfect for front edges and mixed containers. Medium-blue tufts cover the foliage all season; shear lightly if bloom slows in heat.

planting.note: The white form of the dwarf Cloud Nine series, planted after frost.

directions: Press seed onto warm mix and leave it uncovered so light can reach it. This dwarf selection carries soft white tufts on low, uniform plants for tidy edging. Remove faded heads to keep the display clean and blooming through fall.

planting.note: A mid-height, mounding F1 selection set out after frost.

directions: Start seed on the surface of warm, moist mix under bright light. This selection builds a full, mounding plant that holds up well in landscape beds. Deep-blue tufts stay showy all summer and rebloom without heavy deadheading.

planting.note: The white-flowered form of the mounding High Tide selection.

directions: Press seed onto warm mix and keep bright and evenly moist until it sprouts. This mounding selection carries clean white tufts that pair well with blue forms in beds. It blooms steadily through summer and needs only light tidying to look fresh.

planting.note: A bicolor selection in the Patina series planted after frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors in warmth and light, keeping the mix evenly moist. This selection is grown for florets shaded blue and white for a soft two-tone look. Deadhead spent clusters to keep the bicolor effect crisp through the season.

planting.note: A compact, early-flowering selection set out once frost has passed.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of warm, moist mix and leave it exposed to light. This compact, early selection forms a dense low mound of rich blue tufts. It flowers young and keeps going all summer with only light shearing.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in late spring or midsummer for bloom the following year.

directions: Press the flat seeds barely under the soil surface, as light aids germination, and keep the bed evenly moist. Grow the first-year rosette in a sunny, sheltered spot and stake the tall bloom spike the second summer. Expect towers of single funnel-shaped flowers to open from the bottom up in early to midsummer of the second year.

planting.note: Sow in late spring where plants are to grow; blooms the second season.

directions: Sow just beneath the surface into well-drained soil and thin seedlings to stand about two feet apart. Valued for its distinctive fig-shaped leaves and noticeably better resistance to hollyhock rust than common hollyhock. Single satiny flowers in warm shades line the tall stems from early summer onward.

planting.note: Sow late spring to midsummer for pale-yellow bloom the following year.

directions: Barely cover the seed and give it a hot, sunny site with sharp drainage where it tolerates dry spells well. This rugged species offers soft lemon-yellow single flowers and unusually strong rust resistance. Watch the wandlike stems bloom in early to midsummer and self-sow gently if seed heads are left to ripen.

planting.note: Direct sow in a warm border for second-year flowering.

directions: Sow shallowly into lean, well-drained ground and thin to give each plant room to form its rosette. This drought-tolerant species carries soft pink to mauve single flowers with a deeper eye. Tall spikes open in early summer of the second year and are attractive to bees.

planting.note: Sow late spring for dramatic near-black blooms the second year.

directions: Set seed just below the surface in full sun and stake the tall stems as buds form. The single flowers open a deep chocolate-maroon so dark they read as black against a yellow center. Blooms climb the spike from early to midsummer of the second season.

planting.note: Sow late spring to midsummer for fully double blooms the following year.

directions: Sow shallowly and grow the rosettes in rich, well-drained soil with full sun. This old strain produces peony-like fully double flowers in a mix of pink, red, white, yellow and maroon. The packed rosettes of petals open along tall spikes in the second summer.

planting.note: Sow in early summer for double maroon flowers the next year.

directions: Press seed lightly into moist soil and site in full sun with shelter from wind. The rich wine-maroon flowers are fully double and ruffled like small peonies. Enjoy the tall spikes blooming from the base upward in midsummer of the second season.

planting.note: Sow late spring for double salmon-pink blooms the following summer.

directions: Sow just beneath the surface where plants will grow and keep evenly watered while young. The warm salmon-pink flowers are densely double and softly ruffled. Tall stems bloom in sequence through the second summer.

planting.note: Sow early summer for pure-white double flowers the next year.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow on in full sun in fertile, free-draining soil. The clean white fully double flowers glow against the tall green spikes and read well at dusk. Blooms open from the bottom of the stem upward in the second summer.

planting.note: Sow late spring for deep violet double blooms the following year.

directions: Sow shallowly and thin to about two feet apart in a sunny border. Rich purple-violet flowers are fully double and ruffled like a small rosette. The tall spikes flower steadily through the second summer.

planting.note: Start early indoors to coax first-year bloom, or direct sow for the following season.

directions: Sow indoors eight weeks before the last frost, barely covering the seed, then harden off and transplant. This old strain bears single and semi-double flowers in rose, pink and white shades. Early sowings often flower the first summer, with fuller displays the year after.

planting.note: Sow late spring for fully double pompon blooms the following year.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm, moist soil in full sun. The fully double, tightly packed flowers resemble powder puffs in a mix of pastel and bold shades. Tall spikes bloom through the second summer and make good cut flowers.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for dwarf, first-year bloom.

directions: Sow indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and barely cover the seed. This compact strain flowers the first year with semi-double, frilled blooms in a bright mix. Its short, sturdy stems rarely need staking and bloom from midsummer.

planting.note: Start early indoors for double blooms the first summer.

directions: Sow indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, keeping seed just under the surface. This award-winning strain flowers the first year with large double blooms in mixed colors that begin low on the stem. Expect flowering from early summer if started early enough.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for compact first-year flowering.

directions: Sow indoors barely covered and transplant after frost into full sun. This dwarf, award-winning selection flowers the first year with fully double, rich purple pompons. Its short stature suits containers and the front of a border, blooming from midsummer.

planting.note: Sow late spring for single flowers with a contrasting eye the next year.

directions: Barely cover the seed in full sun and thin seedlings to stand well apart. Single soft-blush flowers carry a deep magenta central halo that draws in pollinators. Tall spikes bloom from early summer of the second year.

planting.note: Sow early summer for bright single flowers the following year.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and grow on in a sunny, sheltered site. Single scarlet-red flowers show a darker eye at the center that pollinators find irresistible. Bloom spikes rise and open in early summer of the second season.

planting.note: Sow late spring for single cerise flowers the following summer.

directions: Sow just beneath the surface in full sun and keep the young rosettes weed-free. Vivid cerise-pink single flowers carry a deep eye that gives the series its name. Tall stems bloom from the base upward in the second summer.

planting.note: Sow early summer for soft lavender single blooms the next year.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow in full sun with good drainage. Pale lavender single flowers are lit by a rich purple central halo. The tall spikes flower steadily from early summer of the second year.

planting.note: Sow late spring for warm apricot single flowers the next year.

directions: Sow shallowly in full sun and thin to give each plant space to develop. Soft apricot single flowers glow around a deep maroon eye. Tall stems bloom through midsummer of the second season and attract bees.

planting.note: Sow early summer for crisp white single flowers the following year.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface in full sun and keep evenly moist. Clean white single flowers are centered by a striking dark eye. The bloom spikes open from early summer of the second year.

planting.note: Sow late spring for creamy single flowers the following summer.

directions: Sow just under the soil in full sun and thin the seedlings once established. Pale cream single flowers surround a deep wine-red central halo. Tall stems bloom from the base upward through the second summer.

planting.note: Sow early summer for pink-and-eye single flowers the next year.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow the rosettes in full sun. Bright candy-pink single flowers are marked by a deep contrasting eye. The tall spikes bloom in early to midsummer of the second season.

planting.note: Sow late spring for two-tone semi-double flowers the following year.

directions: Sow shallowly into fertile soil in full sun and stake the tall stems as they extend. The single to semi-double flowers blend deep blackcurrant-purple with paler cream reverses, so no two look quite alike. Blooms open along the spike in the second summer.

planting.note: Sow early summer for ruffled peach double flowers the next year.

directions: Press seed lightly into moist soil in full sun and thin to about two feet apart. The fully double, ruffled flowers open in soft peach with pink-blushed centers. Tall stems flower from the base upward in the second summer.

planting.note: Sow late spring for fringed double flowers the following year.

directions: Barely cover the seed in a sunny, sheltered bed and stake as the spikes rise. The frilled, fringed double flowers open deep blackcurrant-purple with paler edges. Enjoy the ruffled blooms climbing the stem in the second summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for dwarf first-year bloom.

directions: Sow indoors about eight weeks before the last frost and barely cover the seed. This compact strain flowers the first year with frilled semi-double blooms edged in a lighter picotee. Its short stems suit pots and the front of the border and bloom from midsummer.

planting.note: Start early indoors for dwarf, first-year double flowers.

directions: Sow indoors barely covered eight to ten weeks before the last frost, then transplant after frost. This dwarf series flowers reliably the first year with fully double soft-lemon blooms. Its stout stems seldom need staking and flower from early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for dwarf first-year flowering.

directions: Sow indoors and barely cover the seed, transplanting seedlings after the last frost. This compact selection bears fully double, rich crimson flowers in its first summer. The short, sturdy stems are ideal for containers and small beds.

planting.note: Start early indoors for compact first-year bloom.

directions: Sow indoors barely covered and grow on in bright light before transplanting after frost. This dwarf series flowers the first year with fully double rose-pink blooms. Its short height makes staking unnecessary in most gardens, blooming from midsummer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for dwarf first-year flowering.

directions: Sow indoors and barely cover the seed, hardening off before planting out after frost. This compact strain flowers the first year with fully double, deep purple blooms. The short stems hold up well in wind and suit the front of a sunny border.

planting.note: Start early indoors for dwarf, first-year double flowers.

directions: Sow indoors barely covered eight weeks before the last frost, then transplant once settled weather arrives. This dwarf series flowers the first year with clean, fully double white blooms. Compact and self-supporting, it flowers freely from early summer.

planting.note: Sow late spring for a bright single-flower mix the following year.

directions: Sow just beneath the surface in full sun and thin to stand about two feet apart. This lively strain produces large single flowers in a vivid mix of warm and bright shades. Tall spikes bloom from the base upward in the second summer and draw pollinators.

planting.note: Sow early summer for vivid single red flowers the following year.

directions: Barely cover the seed in full sun and stake the tall stems as buds set. The large single flowers open a bright fiery red that stands out from a distance. Bloom spikes flower from early summer of the second season.

planting.note: Sow late spring for large white single flowers the next year.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil in full sun and keep the rosettes weed-free. The large single flowers open pure white with a soft pale-yellow throat. Tall spikes bloom from early summer of the second year and light up the border.

planting.note: Sow late spring for tall pale-yellow single flowers the next year.

directions: Sow shallowly in full sun with good drainage and give the very tall stems firm staking. This robust hybrid carries single, soft primrose-yellow flowers on towering spikes. Enjoy the stately bloom from early summer of the second year, often persisting a few seasons.

planting.note: Sow late spring for rosy single flowers on tall stems the next year.

directions: Barely cover the seed in a sunny, well-drained bed and stake the tall stems early. This vigorous hybrid bears single rose-pink flowers on statuesque spikes. Blooms climb the stem from early summer of the second season and may return for a few years.

planting.note: Sow late spring for a single-flower mix on rust-resistant plants the next year.

directions: Sow just under the surface in full sun and thin seedlings to about two feet apart. This fig-leaved strain offers single flowers in a broad mix of colors on plants with strong rust resistance. Tall spikes bloom from early summer of the second year and reseed gently.

planting.note: Sow late spring for very dark single flowers the following year.

directions: Press seed lightly into the soil in full sun and stake the tall stems as they rise. The single flowers open a deep chocolate-maroon so dark they appear nearly black. Bloom spikes flower from the base upward in the second summer.

planting.note: Direct sow once frost has passed and nights stay mild.

directions: Press the seed lightly into warm soil and keep it damp during germination. Space plants so air moves freely between them and water during dry spells. This form drapes chartreuse-green ropes instead of red, prized fresh or dried by late summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after the frost date on warmed ground.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the bed moist until seedlings emerge in a week or two. Grow in full sun with steady moisture for the fullest tassels. The soft coral-pink cascades spill downward from midsummer and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when soil reaches the upper sixties.

directions: Sow shallowly on warm soil and thin the young plants to a hand's width apart. Give full sun and even watering to build the heavy flower heads. This selection forms unusually thick, upright rose-red plumes that ripen in late summer.

planting.note: Sow when spring frosts are over and soil has warmed.

directions: Cover seed with just a dusting of soil and keep it moist through sprouting. Plant in full sun and water when the top inch dries. The wine-red rope-like tassels twist and dangle from midsummer, holding well as cut or dried stems.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost on warm ground.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface of warm soil and keep it evenly moist. Thin seedlings and grow in full sun for the deepest color. Long blood-red tassels trail from summer into autumn and dry cleanly for crafts.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once nights are reliably above freezing.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly and firm it against warm, moist soil. Grow in full sun and thin to prevent crowding of the tall stems. Upright feathery flower plumes rise from midsummer in shades of red and bronze.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier.

directions: Scatter seed on warm soil, barely cover, and keep moist until it sprouts. Give full sun and room for the tall stalks, staking in exposed spots. The branching plumes turn a warm amber-brown in late summer and are excellent dried.

planting.note: Sow after the frost date when soil is warm.

directions: Sow shallowly into warm ground and keep the surface damp during germination. Thin plants and grow in full sun with moderate water. Dark maroon foliage carries deep burgundy upright plumes that mature by late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or transplant started seedlings out.

directions: Barely bury the seed in warm soil and keep evenly moist until emergence. Grow in full sun and thin to avoid crowding the tall stems. Plumes open in a mix of amber, russet, and gold tones during late summer, ideal for drying.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the soil warms after the last frost.

directions: Sow the small seed shallowly on warm soil and keep it moist to sprout. Give full sun and space the vigorous plants generously. Intense crimson-magenta foliage and plumes develop through summer; the pigment has long been used as a natural dye.

planting.note: Wait until the soil is thoroughly warm before sowing.

directions: Sow into warm soil and cover lightly, as this species is slow to start when cool. Grow in full sun where the leaf color develops most vividly. It is grown for its brilliant red, yellow, and green upper foliage rather than its flowers, peaking in late summer.

planting.note: Sow or set out after all frost when soil is warm.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil or start indoors and transplant after frost. Plant in full sun for the strongest coloring and pinch nothing back. The upper crown blazes fiery crimson above bronze-green lower leaves by late summer.

planting.note: Sow after frost into warm ground for best establishment.

directions: Barely cover the seed in warm soil and keep it moist until sprouting. Grow in full sun where heat brings out the leaf pigments. The crown lights up scarlet and copper over deep bronze foliage in the heat of summer.

planting.note: Sow when soil is warm after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow the tiny seed shallowly on warm soil and keep it damp to germinate. Site in full sun to bring out the brightest tones. The top leaves turn a clear golden-yellow above green foliage, and young leaves are also edible.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or transplant into warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly into warm soil and thin seedlings once established. Grow in full sun and water steadily through hot weather. The upper foliage colors in red, gold, and green tiers, brightest as summer peaks.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after the frost date on warmed soil.

directions: Scatter seed thinly on warm soil, barely cover, and keep it moist. Grow in full sun and thin to give the tall stalks space. Erect crimson-purple flower plumes rise from midsummer above bronze-tinged leaves.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow shallowly on warm soil and keep moist until seedlings appear. This compact selection needs full sun but little staking. Short, stiff upright maroon plumes stand above dark foliage from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the bed moist through germination. Grow in full sun and thin to a hand's width apart. This dwarf form carries upright bright-green plumes, prized fresh and dried, from midsummer on.

planting.note: Sow after frost, or start indoors and transplant to warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly into warm soil and thin the strong seedlings well apart. Grow in full sun with room for the large plants. Each stalk forms a thick, drooping purple-maroon flower head shaped like an elephant's trunk by late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost onto warm, moist soil.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and keep the surface damp to sprout. Grow in full sun and thin the tall plants for airflow. Narrow upright scarlet-red plumes rise well above the foliage through late summer.

planting.note: Sow after frost once the ground is warm.

directions: Sow the seed shallowly on warm soil and thin the vigorous seedlings generously. Grow in full sun and stake the very tall stalks in wind. Large golden-blond seed heads top the plants in late summer and yield abundant edible grain.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil warms to the upper sixties.

directions: Barely cover the seed on warm soil and keep it moist to germinate. Grow in full sun with steady water for long tassels. Soft lime-green ropes cascade downward from midsummer, holding color as cut or dried stems.

planting.note: Direct sow after the frost date onto warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly on warm soil and keep the bed damp until seedlings show. Grow in full sun and thin the tall stems for good airflow. Deep bronze-red foliage and matching plumes develop through late summer and dry attractively.

planting.note: Sow in autumn or very early spring; cool starts produce sturdier, better-branched plants.

directions: Press seed into the surface and cover lightly, watering gently to avoid washing it away. Give the plants room and full sun to develop their sturdy stems and finely cut foliage, and avoid overfeeding which encourages flop. Harvest the domed, honeydew-green umbels as they firm up and begin to pale toward cream.

planting.note: Start indoors in cool conditions or direct sow early; this cut-flower strain is bred for long, strong stems.

directions: Sow shallowly and grow on cool to keep plants stocky before setting out after hard frost passes. Space generously and net or corral the tall stems, which are selected for uniform height and clean white lacework prized by florists. Cut when most florets in a head have opened for the longest vase life.

planting.note: A tall cut-flower selection; start early under cool conditions or direct sow in spring.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it lightly moist and cool until germination. Grow in full sun with support, as the large lacy white heads sit atop long stems that can catch the wind. Snip stems for arranging once the umbels are nearly fully open but still fresh.

planting.note: Sow in autumn or early spring; the domed lime-green heads mature to soft white.

directions: Surface-sow and cover only lightly, keeping the bed moist until seedlings emerge. Give plants full sun and space to branch, and stake in exposed spots to keep the sturdy stems upright. Cut the tight, dome-shaped chartreuse umbels as they expand, before they fully bleach to white, for the freshest color.

planting.note: Direct sow early or in fall; selected for sturdy stems and crisp white domed umbels.

directions: Press the seed onto firmed soil and barely cover it, watering gently so it stays in place. Grow in full sun and lean soil for the strongest stems, thinning early to avoid crowding. Harvest the crisp white, tightly domed heads once they have firmed up for reliable cut-flower performance.

planting.note: Sow indoors early; this seed strain flowers without pinching.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a warm tray and keep it bright and evenly damp until sprouts appear. Move seedlings to full sun after frost and feed lightly through the season. Deep violet spikes need no deadheading and keep coming until fall frost.

planting.note: Start seed under lights for the earliest white spikes.

directions: Leave the surface-sown seed uncovered and warm until it germinates in two to three weeks. Harden off and plant in full sun once the soil has warmed. Clean white flower spikes hold up in heat and humidity without staking.

planting.note: An early, well-branched seed strain that blooms without pinching.

directions: Sow on the surface of warm, moist mix and keep bright until germination. Set plants out in full sun after the last frost and water when the top inch dries. Soft lavender spikes bloom steadily and are magnets for bees.

planting.note: Start indoors for compact, uniform bedding plants.

directions: Barely press the fine seed into warm mix and hold moisture with a humidity dome until it sprouts. Transplant to a sunny bed once nights are reliably warm. Two-tone pink spikes bloom nonstop and need no deadheading.

planting.note: Give seedlings a long warm start for full summer spikes.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix and keep it lit and damp through germination. Plant out in full sun after frost danger passes and mulch to hold moisture. Violet-blue spikes shrug off heat and bloom until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for well-branched, self-cleaning plants.

directions: Press seed lightly onto warm, moist mix and leave uncovered for light. Move to full sun after the last frost and feed monthly for steady growth. Clear pink spikes bloom from base to tip and hold through summer heat.

planting.note: Part of a heat-loving seed series for beds and containers.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep warm and bright until seedlings emerge. Plant in full sun once soil is warm and water evenly during dry spells. Deep rose-pink spikes need no pinching and carry color into autumn.

planting.note: A dwarf seed strain that stays compact without growth regulators.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on warm mix and keep it lit and moist until it sprouts. Plant out in full sun after frost; its compact habit rarely needs staking. Rich purple spikes bloom early and stay tidy in small containers.

planting.note: An award-winning compact seed variety for beds and pots.

directions: Press seed onto warm, damp mix and leave uncovered under bright light. Transplant to a sunny spot after the last frost and water when the surface dries. Soft pink spikes bloom heavily on a dwarf, weather-tough plant.

planting.note: Compact seed strain in a deep raspberry shade.

directions: Sow on the surface of warm mix and hold humidity until germination in two to three weeks. Set out in full sun once nights warm and feed lightly for steady bloom. Rosy-raspberry spikes color a low, well-branched mound all summer.

planting.note: Dwarf, naturally compact seed variety for tidy edging.

directions: Barely settle the seed into warm mix and keep bright and evenly moist until it sprouts. Plant out in full sun after frost and space closely for an edging effect. Crisp white spikes stay short and bloom without any deadheading.

planting.note: A soft blue dwarf seed strain that stays compact in heat.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix and keep it lit and damp through germination. Transplant to full sun after the last frost and mulch to conserve moisture. Pale sky-blue spikes bloom on a neat mound and draw in pollinators.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once the soil and nights have warmed.

directions: Plant vegetative starts at the same depth they grew in the pot after all frost has passed. Give full sun, average water, and a monthly feed for tall, sturdy spikes. Deep blue-violet flowers bloom continuously without deadheading right up to frost.

planting.note: A vegetatively grown variety planted from rooted cuttings.

directions: Transplant rooted starts into well-drained soil once the weather is warm and settled. Water at the base and feed lightly to fuel the upright spikes. Pure white flowers open along tall stems and keep going through summer heat.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant out after the danger of frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into warm, sunny beds or roomy containers. Keep evenly moist while establishing, then let it dry slightly between waterings. Bright pink spikes carry color on tall stems from planting through fall.

planting.note: A bicolor vegetative selection set out from rooted starts.

directions: Plant starts after frost in full sun with room for air to circulate. Feed monthly and water steadily during establishment for the fullest spikes. Two-tone blue and white flowers bloom nonstop on strong upright stems.

planting.note: Vegetative variety in a clear pink; plant after frost.

directions: Transplant rooted cuttings into warm, sunny, well-drained soil. Water at the roots and give light monthly feeding for vigorous spikes. Clean pink flowers stack up tall stems and bloom until first frost.
